notes: Subagent duration_ms and total_tokens were not exposed in completion notifications in this environment.
Used output character count as token proxy and set timing fields to 0.

Seven of nine assertions are non-discriminating in this benchmark: all three assertions in eval 0 and eval 1 pass in both configurations, and the '[P] non-overlap rule' assertion in eval 2 also passes in both.
Skill impact is concentrated in eval 2: two assertions ('all five output sections' and 'explicit checkpoint pass/fail gating') pass with skill and fail without skill.
With-skill pass_rate is perfectly stable at 1.0 across all evals, while without-skill variance is high (stddev 0.3868) because eval 0 and eval 1 are perfect but eval 2 drops to 0.33.
No runs report errors in either configuration, so the pass-rate gap is due to unmet expectations rather than execution failures.
Token overhead is not uniform: with-skill uses fewer tokens in eval 0 (-2) and eval 1 (-23), but uses substantially more in eval 2 (+488), producing the overall +154 mean delta.
Token variance is high in both configurations (with-skill stddev 1028.0566, without-skill stddev 778.1422), indicating eval-specific workload differences dominate aggregate token means.
time_seconds and tool_calls are non-informative in this dataset (all 0), so benchmark aggregates cannot support runtime or tool-usage comparisons for this iteration.
Benchmark metadata lists runs_per_configuration=3, but each eval/configuration appears once; this is consistent with three evals per configuration rather than repeated stochastic runs.
